metadata.title: Economic Consequences of Recurrent Lumbar Disc Herniation: A Systematic Review of Cost-Effectiveness Analyses
authors: Not explicitly stated in provided text
metadata.journal: Not explicitly stated in provided text
metadata.abstract: Recurrent lumbar disc herniation (RLDH) is a common complication after discectomy, occurring in 2%-25% of patients and contributing to higher reoperation rates, reduced satisfaction, and substantial direct and indirect costs. This review evaluates the economic consequences of RLDH and the relative cost-effectiveness of available management strategies. A systematic search of OVID, MEDLINE, and the Cochrane Library was performed through August 2025. Peer-reviewed, English-language studies were included if they examined adults (≥18 years) with RLDH and reported economic data. Exclusion criteria were studies limited to primary, cervical, or thoracic herniations; animal or cadaveric models; and abstracts. Extracted variables included study design, sample size, follow-up duration, and cost components. Of 283 records identified, 220 were screened and 35 underwent full-text review. Six studies met inclusion criteria, with 2 added through citation searching. Reported costs varied considerably: repeat discectomy added $6,907 in one analysis, while fusion increased expenses by more than 350%. Across studies, repeat discectomy remained the most cost-efficient option, providing comparable outcomes with reduced perioperative expenditures. Conservative management had the lowest immediate direct costs (≈$2,300) but likely underestimates the overall burden due to unmeasured productivity losses. Annular closure devices demonstrated potential cost savings of $2,000-5,000 over 2-5 years. RLDH imposes a substantial economic burden. Heterogeneity in costing methods remains a major limitation which hinders evidence-based determinations. Greater transparency, methodological standardization, and incorporation of societal perspectives are essential to accurately assess the socioeconomic impact of RLDH.

spine_metadata.main_conclusion: Repeat discectomy remains the most cost-efficient management option for RLDH with comparable outcomes and reduced perioperative costs, while standardization of costing methodologies is needed for accurate economic assessment.

pico.population: Adults (≥18 years) with recurrent lumbar disc herniation
pico.intervention: Repeat discectomy, fusion, annular closure devices, conservative management
pico.outcome: Direct costs, perioperative expenditures, cost-effectiveness, long-term economic burden

effect_measure.label: Annular closure device cost savings: $2,000-5,000 (2-5 years)

chunks.content: Recurrent lumbar disc herniation (RLDH) is a common complication after discectomy, occurring in 2%-25% of patients and contributing to higher reoperation rates, reduced satisfaction, and substantial direct and indirect costs.
chunks.summary: RLDH occurs in 2%-25% of post-discectomy patients and causes high reoperation rates, reduced satisfaction, and significant economic burden.

chunks.content: This review evaluates the economic consequences of RLDH and the relative cost-effectiveness of available management strategies.
chunks.summary: Systematic review examining economic consequences and cost-effectiveness of RLDH management strategies.

chunks.content: A systematic search of OVID, MEDLINE, and the Cochrane Library was performed through August 2025. Peer-reviewed, English-language studies were included if they examined adults (≥18 years) with RLDH and reported economic data. Exclusion criteria were studies limited to primary, cervical, or thoracic herniations; animal or cadaveric models; and abstracts.
chunks.summary: Systematic search of three major databases through August 2025 with inclusion criteria for adult RLDH studies with economic data.

chunks.content: Extracted variables included study design, sample size, follow-up duration, and cost components.
chunks.summary: Data extraction protocol included study characteristics and cost-related variables.

chunks.content: Of 283 records identified, 220 were screened and 35 underwent full-text review. Six studies met inclusion criteria, with 2 added through citation searching.
chunks.summary: Systematic review identified 8 studies (6 from database search, 2 from citations) that met inclusion criteria from 283 initial records.

chunks.content: Reported costs varied considerably: repeat discectomy added $6,907 in one analysis, while fusion increased expenses by more than 350%.
chunks.summary: Wide variation in reported costs with repeat discectomy at $6,907 additional cost and fusion increasing expenses >350%.

chunks.content: Across studies, repeat discectomy remained the most cost-efficient option, providing comparable outcomes with reduced perioperative expenditures.
chunks.summary: Repeat discectomy demonstrated superior cost-efficiency compared to other RLDH management strategies with comparable clinical outcomes.

effect_measure.label: Repeat discectomy: most cost-efficient management strategy

chunks.content: Conservative management had the lowest immediate direct costs (≈$2,300) but likely underestimates the overall burden due to unmeasured productivity losses.
chunks.summary: Conservative management has lowest initial direct costs ($2,300) but underestimates total burden when productivity losses are considered.

chunks.content: Annular closure devices demonstrated potential cost savings of $2,000-5,000 over 2-5 years.
chunks.summary: Annular closure devices show cost savings potential of $2,000-5,000 over 2-5 year follow-up period.

effect_measure.label: Annular closure device cost savings: $2,000-5,000 (2-5 years)

chunks.content: RLDH imposes a substantial economic burden.
chunks.summary: RLDH represents a major economic burden to healthcare systems.

chunks.content: Heterogeneity in costing methods remains a major limitation which hinders evidence-based determinations.
chunks.summary: Methodological heterogeneity in cost analyses limits ability to draw evidence-based conclusions about RLDH management economics.

chunks.content: Greater transparency, methodological standardization, and incorporation of societal perspectives are essential to accurately assess the socioeconomic impact of RLDH.
chunks.summary: Future economic analyses of RLDH require standardized methodology, transparency, and societal perspective to improve evidence quality.
